entry.title: Taming Stale Data: Off-Policy Reinforcement Learning for LLMs with Monotonic Improvement Guarantees

link.title: Taming Stale Data: Off-Policy Reinforcement Learning for LLMs with Monotonic Improvement Guarantees

entry.content:
This post asks a specific question: when a training batch mixes data from multiple historical policy versions, under what conditions can a PPO-style update still retain an analyzable monotonic-improvement lower bound?

1. Introduction: Why Should We Care About “Off-Policy”?

When training a large language model with reinforcement learning, the most direct setup is on-policy training: generate a batch of data, update on that batch, then sample again from the updated model.

In large-scale distributed training, though, hundreds of GPUs sample in parallel and model updates take time. By the time a new version is deployed, data generated by older versions are often still sitting in the queue: throwing them away is wasteful, but using them means training on stale data.

That is the central off-policy question: when can data collected by older policies still support reliable improvement of a newer one?

This post studies exactly that question: under what conditions can a mixture of data from multiple policy versions still support a monotonic-improvement guarantee?

One scope note up front: many LLM-RL workloads are closer to single-episode contextual bandits than to general long-horizon MDPs. I still start from the discounted-MDP setting because it makes the roles of multi-source sampling, staleness, and clipping easiest to state. Section 7 returns to the bandit simplifications.

You can also read this post as a continuation of the earlier three-policy perspective: here the behavior side is no longer a single policy ⟦INLMATH31MATHEND⟧, but a mixture over historical policies ⟦INLMATH32MATHEND⟧, while ⟦INLMATH33MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH34MATHEND⟧ play the roles of the current reference policy and update target.

2. Theoretical Foundations

2.1 Basic Setup

We consider a standard Markov Decision Process (MDP) comprising a state space ⟦INLMATH35MATHEND⟧, action space ⟦INLMATH36MATHEND⟧, transition probability ⟦INLMATH37MATHEND⟧, reward function ⟦INLMATH38MATHEND⟧, initial distribution ⟦INLMATH39MATHEND⟧, and discount factor ⟦INLMATH40MATHEND⟧.

The expected cumulative discounted return of policy ⟦INLMATH41MATHEND⟧ is:

⟦DISPMATH9MATHEND⟧

Discounted State Visitation Distribution

Represents the weighted frequency of visiting each state during long-term policy execution:

⟦DISPMATH10MATHEND⟧

Advantage Function

Measures how much better action ⟦INLMATH42MATHEND⟧ is compared to the policy’s average:

⟦DISPMATH11MATHEND⟧

Total Variation Distance (TV Distance)

Measures the difference between two policies’ action distributions at state ⟦INLMATH43MATHEND⟧:

⟦DISPMATH12MATHEND⟧

Throughout, we use ⟦INLMATH44MATHEND⟧ for conditional probability (e.g., ⟦INLMATH45MATHEND⟧) and reserve ⟦INLMATH46MATHEND⟧ for norms.

2.2 Core Tool: Policy Performance Difference Lemma

The cornerstone of the entire theory is this elegant result:

Lemma 2.1 (Policy Performance Difference Lemma)

For any policies ⟦INLMATH47MATHEND⟧ (old) and ⟦INLMATH48MATHEND⟧ (new), the performance difference can be expressed as:

⟦DISPMATH1MATHEND⟧

Intuitive understanding: How much better the new policy is than the old equals the “average advantage” obtained by selecting actions according to the new policy under the state distribution visited by the new policy.

3. Performance Improvement Bounds for Single-Policy Sampling

3.1 Distribution Mismatch and Controlling State Shift

The Policy Performance Difference Lemma has a practical issue: the expectation on the right-hand side is computed under ⟦INLMATH49MATHEND⟧ (the new policy’s state distribution), while we can only sample from ⟦INLMATH50MATHEND⟧ (the old policy).

The solution is to decompose the expectation into “expectation under the old distribution + bias term,” then control the bias. The key question is: What is the quantitative relationship between the difference in state distributions and the difference in policies?

Controlling State Distribution Differences

Lemma 3.1 (Relationship Between State Distribution Difference and Policy TV Distance)

⟦DISPMATH2MATHEND⟧

This is an average-divergence / average-TV style bound. It is closer in spirit to CPO / Achiam et al. (2017) than to the classic TRPO presentation based on ⟦INLMATH51MATHEND⟧. I use it here because it lands more naturally on sample averages and is easier to carry into the multi-source setting.

Physical Interpretation

Small differences in policies in action space are “amplified” through environment dynamics into differences in state visitation distributions. The coefficient ⟦INLMATH52MATHEND⟧ reflects the temporal accumulation effect—in long-horizon tasks (⟦INLMATH53MATHEND⟧ close to 1), the amplification is stronger.

Proof Sketch

By deriving the fixed-point equation for discounted visitation distributions and exploiting the ⟦INLMATH54MATHEND⟧ non-expansiveness of stochastic matrices, one can show that state-distribution differences are amplified by policy differences through transition dynamics, yielding the bound in Lemma 3.1.

3.2 Policy Performance Improvement Lower Bound

Theorem 3.2 (Policy Performance Improvement Lower Bound)

Define the expected advantage upper bound constant ⟦INLMATH55MATHEND⟧. Then:

⟦DISPMATH3MATHEND⟧

where the surrogate objective is:

⟦DISPMATH4MATHEND⟧

Here ⟦INLMATH56MATHEND⟧ omits the additive constant ⟦INLMATH57MATHEND⟧, so in textbook TRPO notation one could also write the objective as ⟦INLMATH58MATHEND⟧. Also note that ⟦INLMATH59MATHEND⟧ depends on the new policy ⟦INLMATH60MATHEND⟧, so it should be read as a structural coefficient in the bound rather than a tunable hyperparameter.

This lower bound consists of two parts:

  1. Surrogate objective ⟦INLMATH61MATHEND⟧: Can be directly estimated from old policy data via importance sampling; this is the optimization objective of TRPO/PPO.

  2. Policy shift penalty: Increases with the TV distance between new and old policies, explaining why PPO needs to constrain update magnitude.

Core conclusion: this is an explicit improvement lower bound. In particular, when the right-hand side is positive, improvement is guaranteed.

4. Multi-Policy Static Mixture Sampling

4.1 Setup and Unified Modeling (Static Mixture)

In practice, a batch of data may come from multiple policy versions ⟦INLMATH62MATHEND⟧, with respective proportions ⟦INLMATH63MATHEND⟧. How do we extend Theorem 3.2 to this setting?

Core idea: augmented state space

The solution is an elegant modeling technique: treat the policy version index as part of the state.

Define the augmented state space ⟦INLMATH64MATHEND⟧, where ⟦INLMATH65MATHEND⟧ is the policy index set. Under augmented state ⟦INLMATH66MATHEND⟧, the mixture behavior policy is defined as ⟦INLMATH67MATHEND⟧.

The evolution of indices is characterized by the index transition kernel ⟦INLMATH68MATHEND⟧. The augmented MDP inherits the original MDP’s rewards and environment transitions, with indices evolving independently according to ⟦INLMATH69MATHEND⟧.

This technique works because the new policy ⟦INLMATH70MATHEND⟧ has the same return in the augmented MDP as in the original one, so Theorem 3.2 can be applied directly.

4.2 Trajectory-Level Mixture: Simplification and Improvement Bound

The most common scenario is using a single old policy per trajectory: at trajectory start, sample index ⟦INLMATH71MATHEND⟧, and use ⟦INLMATH72MATHEND⟧ throughout. In this case, the index transition kernel is the identity: ⟦INLMATH73MATHEND⟧.

From an engineering perspective, in many actor-learner asynchronous training setups (when sampling and training organize data by “entire trajectories/complete episodes belonging to a certain policy version”), this approximately corresponds to what we call trajectory-level mixture: actors use a fixed policy snapshot within a sampling unit to generate data, while learners mix trajectories from different versions for updates. We say “approximately” because different systems may not have identical boundaries for “trajectory/sampling unit.”

Lemma 4.1 (Structural Simplification for Trajectory-Level Mixture)

(a) The augmented state visitation distribution decomposes as: ⟦INLMATH74MATHEND⟧

(b) The advantage function reduces to: ⟦INLMATH75MATHEND⟧

Intuition for (b): Since the index never changes, all future trajectories starting from augmented state ⟦INLMATH76MATHEND⟧ are generated by the same policy ⟦INLMATH77MATHEND⟧. Therefore, future cumulative returns are entirely determined by ⟦INLMATH78MATHEND⟧, and value functions and advantage functions naturally reduce to their ⟦INLMATH79MATHEND⟧ counterparts.

Consequently, the mixture policy’s return is the weighted average of individual old policies’ returns: ⟦INLMATH80MATHEND⟧.

Improvement bound

Corollary 4.2 (Performance Improvement Lower Bound for Trajectory-Level Mixture)

⟦DISPMATH5MATHEND⟧

This result shows that when mixing trajectories from multiple old policy versions for training, if we construct the loss using importance ratios corresponding to each trajectory’s source policy while controlling the new policy’s deviation from each old policy, the new policy’s performance has a clear improvement lower bound.

Using ⟦INLMATH81MATHEND⟧ is just a compact way to write the result. A slightly tighter version would keep a separate ⟦INLMATH82MATHEND⟧ for each component and then average them with the mixture weights.

5. Dynamic Mixture Sampling and Monotonic Improvement Conditions

5.1 Problem and Unified Modeling (Dynamic Mixture)

Section 4 discussed static mixture—where mixture weights ⟦INLMATH83MATHEND⟧ remain fixed. This section considers the more general dynamic mixture—where sampling gradually transitions to the new policy after it is released.

The previous results characterize improvement of “the new policy relative to the mixture behavior policy.” However, in actual training, what we truly care about is: Does the latest policy ⟦INLMATH84MATHEND⟧ after each update monotonically improve over the previous latest policy ⟦INLMATH85MATHEND⟧?

⟦DISPMATH13MATHEND⟧

Unified Modeling Framework

Two typical forms of dynamic mixture sampling can be uniformly characterized by the index transition kernel ⟦INLMATH86MATHEND⟧:

Trajectory-level mixture (can be viewed as an abstraction of conventional asynchronous training; identity index transition): ⟦INLMATH87MATHEND⟧

Step/segment-level mixture (an abstraction of partial rollout / segment-based sampling; allows switching): ⟦INLMATH88MATHEND⟧

where ⟦INLMATH89MATHEND⟧ is the switching probability and ⟦INLMATH90MATHEND⟧ is the target index distribution.

5.2 Decomposition and Monotonic Improvement Bound

By introducing the mixture return ⟦INLMATH91MATHEND⟧ as an intermediate bridge, the performance difference decomposes as:

⟦DISPMATH14MATHEND⟧

The first term is handled using Theorem 3.2. The second is the mixture bias term. One way to see it is to expand ⟦INLMATH92MATHEND⟧ into a weighted sum of ⟦INLMATH93MATHEND⟧ terms and then bound each component via TV distance. The resulting inequality is:

⟦DISPMATH15MATHEND⟧

Monotonic Improvement Bound

Combining the above results yields the core theorem:

Theorem 5.1 (Monotonic Improvement Lower Bound Under Dynamic Mixture Sampling)

⟦DISPMATH6MATHEND⟧

Here ⟦INLMATH94MATHEND⟧ denotes the surrogate objective relative to the behavior policy ⟦INLMATH95MATHEND⟧ (the same shape as ⟦INLMATH96MATHEND⟧ in Section 3, but with the behavior policy generalized from a single ⟦INLMATH97MATHEND⟧ to the mixture ⟦INLMATH98MATHEND⟧).

More explicitly, one can write

⟦DISPMATH16MATHEND⟧

Similarly, define

⟦DISPMATH17MATHEND⟧

This lower bound contains two penalties, so it naturally points to two separate control problems:

  • Update shift penalty: Deviation of the new policy ⟦INLMATH99MATHEND⟧ from the sampling source policy ⟦INLMATH100MATHEND⟧
  • Sampling staleness penalty: Staleness of the sampling source policy ⟦INLMATH101MATHEND⟧ relative to the current policy ⟦INLMATH102MATHEND⟧

5.3 Why Direct Constraints Are Infeasible: Triangle Inequality Decomposition

The update-shift penalty in Theorem 5.1 may look controllable through constraints on ⟦INLMATH103MATHEND⟧, but that interpretation quickly runs into a practical infeasibility:

Observation 5.2 (Infeasibility of Update Shift Constraints)

Suppose the mixture sampling includes two old policies ⟦INLMATH104MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH105MATHEND⟧. If there exists some state ⟦INLMATH106MATHEND⟧ such that ⟦INLMATH107MATHEND⟧, then no policy ⟦INLMATH108MATHEND⟧ can simultaneously satisfy ⟦INLMATH109MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH110MATHEND⟧.

Proof

By the triangle inequality, if both constraints were satisfied, then ⟦INLMATH111MATHEND⟧, a contradiction.

Root Cause

The update shift penalty directly couples ⟦INLMATH112MATHEND⟧ with the historical policy family ⟦INLMATH113MATHEND⟧, whose internal structure is a product of historical training and not controllable by the current update.

Triangle Inequality Decomposition

The solution leverages the triangle inequality of TV distance:

⟦DISPMATH18MATHEND⟧

This decomposes the coupled constraint into two independent parts:

  • Update increment shift ⟦INLMATH114MATHEND⟧: Deviation of the new policy from the current policy, controllable by the optimization side
  • Sampling staleness ⟦INLMATH115MATHEND⟧: Deviation of the current policy from each old policy, must be controlled by the sampling side

Define:

⟦DISPMATH19MATHEND⟧

Corollary 5.3 (Decomposed Monotonic Improvement Lower Bound)

⟦DISPMATH7MATHEND⟧

Why Does Decomposition Solve the Problem?

The key is that after decomposition, ⟦INLMATH116MATHEND⟧ only involves the new policy ⟦INLMATH117MATHEND⟧ and the current policy ⟦INLMATH118MATHEND⟧, completely independent of the structure of the old policy family ⟦INLMATH119MATHEND⟧. So no matter how far apart the old policies are, constraining ⟦INLMATH120MATHEND⟧ remains feasible. This is exactly how the infeasibility in Observation 5.2 is avoided.

Operationally, this leads to a simple separation of concerns:

Control Term Responsible Party Control Mechanism
⟦INLMATH121MATHEND⟧ (update increment shift) Optimization algorithm Policy clipping
⟦INLMATH122MATHEND⟧ (sampling staleness) Sampling system Data filtering, version window

6. Theoretical Foundations of Clipping Mechanisms

6.1 From TV Distance to Sample-Controllable Quantities

Corollary 5.3 tells us that to guarantee monotonic improvement, we need to control the update increment shift ⟦INLMATH123MATHEND⟧. But TV distance is a distribution-level quantity, so how do we control it with samples?

The bridge from theory to samples is the following identity:

Lemma 6.1 (Ratio Difference Representation of TV Distance)

Suppose policy ⟦INLMATH124MATHEND⟧’s support covers the supports of ⟦INLMATH125MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH126MATHEND⟧. Then for any state distribution ⟦INLMATH127MATHEND⟧:

⟦DISPMATH8MATHEND⟧

Intuitive Understanding

The left side is the TV distance between two distributions (requiring enumeration over all actions), while the right side is the absolute difference of two importance ratios when sampling under ⟦INLMATH128MATHEND⟧. This enables us to estimate and control TV distance using samples.

Sample Representation of ⟦INLMATH129MATHEND⟧

Using Lemma 6.1, setting ⟦INLMATH130MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH131MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH132MATHEND⟧ (the sampling-source policy), we obtain:

⟦DISPMATH20MATHEND⟧

Denoting ⟦INLMATH133MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH134MATHEND⟧, we have:

⟦DISPMATH21MATHEND⟧

This means: If we can ensure ⟦INLMATH135MATHEND⟧ for each sample, we can guarantee ⟦INLMATH136MATHEND⟧.

6.2 Constraining ⟦INLMATH137MATHEND⟧: Two Clipping Options

Method 1: Direct Constraint on Ratio Difference

For each sample ⟦INLMATH138MATHEND⟧, require:

⟦DISPMATH22MATHEND⟧

The clipping interval is ⟦INLMATH139MATHEND⟧, with clipping center at ⟦INLMATH140MATHEND⟧ rather than 1.

Method 2: Constraint on Incremental Ratio

Noting that ⟦INLMATH141MATHEND⟧, we have:

⟦DISPMATH23MATHEND⟧

If we constrain ⟦INLMATH142MATHEND⟧, then

⟦DISPMATH24MATHEND⟧

Taking expectations gives ⟦INLMATH143MATHEND⟧, hence ⟦INLMATH144MATHEND⟧.

This method clips ⟦INLMATH145MATHEND⟧ with center at 1, meaning the clipping constraint itself does not depend on the old policy family ⟦INLMATH146MATHEND⟧. However, if we use the weighted advantage ⟦INLMATH147MATHEND⟧ below, we still need per-sample behavior probabilities (or recorded logprobs) to compute ⟦INLMATH148MATHEND⟧.

Before writing down the clipped objectives, one caveat is worth making explicit: the two inequalities above are hard per-sample constraints at the theory level. The clipped surrogates below are practical approximations meant to keep ⟦INLMATH149MATHEND⟧ in a manageable range; they are not literal guarantees that every optimization step exactly satisfies the hard constraint.

Objective Functions (Three Clipping Mechanisms)

For comparison, we present the complete objective functions for three clipping mechanisms. Suppose the current sample comes from old policy ⟦INLMATH150MATHEND⟧, and denote:

  • ⟦INLMATH151MATHEND⟧ (new policy’s ratio relative to sampling policy)
  • ⟦INLMATH152MATHEND⟧ (current policy’s ratio relative to sampling policy)
  • ⟦INLMATH153MATHEND⟧ (new policy’s incremental ratio relative to current policy)

Note: under trajectory-level mixture (index fixed), ⟦INLMATH154MATHEND⟧, so per-trajectory advantages from the corresponding old policy are consistent; under step/segment-level mixture, replacing ⟦INLMATH155MATHEND⟧ with ⟦INLMATH156MATHEND⟧ introduces advantage-substitution bias (discussed in Section 7), so the advantage/value estimator must reflect future index switching.

Standard PPO (Trajectory-Level Mixture)

Clip ⟦INLMATH157MATHEND⟧ with center at 1

⟦DISPMATH25MATHEND⟧

Method 1

Clip ⟦INLMATH158MATHEND⟧ with center at ⟦INLMATH159MATHEND⟧

⟦DISPMATH26MATHEND⟧

Method 2

Clip incremental ratio ⟦INLMATH160MATHEND⟧ with center at 1

⟦DISPMATH27MATHEND⟧

where ⟦INLMATH161MATHEND⟧ is the importance-weighted advantage estimate.

6.3 Comparison and Practical Controls

Table 6.1 Comparison of Three Clipping Mechanisms

Method Clipped Variable Clipping Center Clipping Interval Constrained TV Distance
Standard PPO ⟦INLMATH162MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH163MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH164MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH165MATHEND⟧
Method 1 ⟦INLMATH166MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH167MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH168MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH169MATHEND⟧
Method 2 ⟦INLMATH170MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH171MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH172MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH173MATHEND⟧

The Fundamental Problem with Standard PPO Under Multi-Policy Mixture

Standard PPO constrains ⟦INLMATH174MATHEND⟧, requiring the new policy to be close to every sampling-source policy at once. By Observation 5.2, when the old policies ⟦INLMATH175MATHEND⟧ differ substantially, no ⟦INLMATH176MATHEND⟧ can satisfy all of those constraints simultaneously. The result is a trust-region intersection that shrinks, or even becomes empty, with the most stale policy becoming the bottleneck.

Common Advantages of Methods 1 and 2

Both methods constrain ⟦INLMATH177MATHEND⟧—the deviation of the new policy from the current policy (rather than the sampling policy). Since ⟦INLMATH178MATHEND⟧ is uniquely determined, this constraint is consistent across all sample sources, completely avoiding the infeasibility problem.

Method 1 vs Method 2

Comparison Dimension Method 1 (Adaptive Clipping) Method 2 (Incremental Clipping)
Stale samples (⟦INLMATH179MATHEND⟧) Automatically tightens constraints, more conservative May produce large gradient variance
LLM large vocabulary low-probability tokens Allows larger absolute changes (additive) Absolute changes are limited (multiplicative)
Implementation complexity Requires storing ⟦INLMATH180MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH181MATHEND⟧ Needs ⟦INLMATH182MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH183MATHEND⟧ (or stored logprobs) to compute ⟦INLMATH184MATHEND⟧; clipping itself uses only ⟦INLMATH185MATHEND⟧
Advantage function Uses ⟦INLMATH186MATHEND⟧ Uses weighted advantage ⟦INLMATH187MATHEND⟧

Detailed Explanations

(1) Handling Stale Samples

When samples come from very old policies, ⟦INLMATH188MATHEND⟧ can be large.

  • Method 2’s integrand is ⟦INLMATH189MATHEND⟧; even if ⟦INLMATH190MATHEND⟧, the integrand can reach ⟦INLMATH191MATHEND⟧, producing spikes.
  • Method 1 directly constrains ⟦INLMATH192MATHEND⟧; the integrand’s upper bound is always ⟦INLMATH193MATHEND⟧, unaffected by ⟦INLMATH194MATHEND⟧ amplification.

(2) LLM Large Vocabulary Issue

Large language models have many tokens having very small probabilities.

  • Method 2 constrains ⟦INLMATH195MATHEND⟧, which is a multiplicative constraint: if ⟦INLMATH196MATHEND⟧, the allowed absolute change is only ⟦INLMATH197MATHEND⟧.
  • Method 1 constrains ⟦INLMATH198MATHEND⟧, which is an additive constraint: if that token has higher probability under the old policy (e.g., ⟦INLMATH199MATHEND⟧), even if the current probability is very low, faster improvement is allowed - provided the token still has enough observable mass under the sampling policy.

Controlling Sampling Staleness

Corollary 5.3 shows that ⟦INLMATH200MATHEND⟧ also enters the monotonic-improvement lower bound, but it cannot be controlled from the optimization side. It has to be handled by the sampling system:

(1) Discarding Stale Data

Set a threshold ⟦INLMATH201MATHEND⟧. For each sample, compute ⟦INLMATH202MATHEND⟧, and discard samples exceeding the threshold.

(2) Controlling Policy Version Window

Limit the number of old policy versions in the mixture sampling, e.g., using only data from the most recent ⟦INLMATH203MATHEND⟧ versions.

Operational Meaning of Clipping

Finally, we clarify the relationship between clipping and the theoretical lower bound.

In Corollary 5.3, the coefficient of ⟦INLMATH204MATHEND⟧, namely ⟦INLMATH205MATHEND⟧, depends on the new policy ⟦INLMATH206MATHEND⟧, so the penalty term cannot simply be replaced by a fixed constant. Operationally, the clipped objective should be read as an approximation to a constrained update, not as a literal theorem with a hand-picked scalar penalty:

Maximize the surrogate objective ⟦INLMATH207MATHEND⟧ subject to the constraint ⟦INLMATH208MATHEND⟧

The clipping objective can be read as a practical approximation to this constrained optimization: clipping keeps the update magnitude in check so that ⟦INLMATH209MATHEND⟧ stays manageable, and then gradient ascent pushes up the surrogate objective within that approximation.

Section Summary

This section established the theoretical foundations of clipping mechanisms:

  1. Lemma 6.1 converts TV distance to sample-level ratio differences, serving as the bridge between theory and implementation
  2. Two constraint methods: Method 1 (adaptive clipping center) and Method 2 (fixed incremental clipping) correspond to two different ways of controlling ⟦INLMATH210MATHEND⟧
  3. Comparison with standard PPO: Standard PPO constrains ⟦INLMATH211MATHEND⟧, which becomes structurally problematic under multi-policy mixture; Methods 1/2 instead target ⟦INLMATH212MATHEND⟧
  4. Method selection: Method 1 (adaptive) is recommended for high staleness or LLM large vocabulary scenarios; Method 2 (incremental) is attractive when you want the trust-region/clipping constraint to avoid depending on the old policy family (but data still needs to provide behavior logprobs to compute ⟦INLMATH213MATHEND⟧)
  5. ⟦INLMATH214MATHEND⟧ control is the sampling side’s responsibility, implemented through data filtering and version windows
  6. Clipping is constrained optimization: Maximize the surrogate objective subject to ⟦INLMATH215MATHEND⟧ constraints

7. Comparison of Trajectory-Level and Step/Segment-Level Mixture

7.1 Mechanism Differences and Estimation Implications

The essential difference between the two mixture mechanisms lies in the structure of the index transition kernel:

  • Trajectory-level mixture: ⟦INLMATH216MATHEND⟧, index never changes
  • Step/segment-level mixture: ⟦INLMATH217MATHEND⟧, allows within-trajectory switching

The correspondence with common engineering terminology is:

  • Trajectory-level mixture here can be roughly understood as an idealized abstraction of “conventional asynchronous training”: data is organized by entire trajectories/episodes belonging to a certain policy version;
  • Step/segment-level mixture here can be roughly understood as an abstraction of “partial rollout”: due to asynchrony between actors and learners, and possible refresh to new policy versions at segment boundaries, using an index transition kernel that allows “within-trajectory version switching” can better approximate this phenomenon.

The key dividing line is whether Lemma 4.1’s structural simplification holds: trajectory-level mixture satisfies the advantage reduction, while step/segment-level mixture generally does not because future returns depend on the index transition kernel.

Differences in Sampling Staleness ⟦INLMATH218MATHEND⟧

Trajectory-level mixture’s staleness arises from: mixture weights ⟦INLMATH219MATHEND⟧ retaining probability mass on old policies after new policy release.

Step/segment-level mixture has an exponential compression effect: Consider a simplified model with switching probability ⟦INLMATH220MATHEND⟧ from old to new. The marginal probability mass on old indices under the discounted visitation distribution is ⟦INLMATH221MATHEND⟧. As long as ⟦INLMATH222MATHEND⟧, the old policy weight can be significantly compressed.

Differences in Surrogate Objective Estimation

Trajectory-level mixture: The advantage function reduces to ⟦INLMATH223MATHEND⟧, with a clear estimation path.

Advantage substitution bias in step/segment-level mixture: If single-policy advantage estimates are used, systematic bias will arise. The reason is that ⟦INLMATH224MATHEND⟧ requires taking expectations over future index switching, while ⟦INLMATH225MATHEND⟧ implicitly assumes “the future always follows ⟦INLMATH226MATHEND⟧.”

Unification Under Bandit Setting

In single-step episode LLM training, with no subsequent state transitions, the estimation problems of both mechanisms unify, with no such bias.

7.2 Risks and Applicable Scenarios

Step/segment-level mixture has another hidden concern: even if single-step importance ratios are clipped, multi-step noise accumulation over long trajectories can still amplify gradient estimation variance. When policy changes per update are large, “behavioral discontinuities” within trajectories may induce heavier-tailed ratio distributions. This is why trajectory-level mixture is recommended for “large policy change per update” scenarios in the table below.

Applicable Scenarios

Table 7.1 Applicable Scenarios for Two Mixture Mechanisms

Scenario Characteristics Recommended Mechanism Rationale
Long trajectories, high-frequency updates, strong asynchrony Step/segment-level Can significantly compress ⟦INLMATH227MATHEND⟧
Short trajectories (non-bandit) Trajectory-level ⟦INLMATH228MATHEND⟧ is naturally low
Large policy change per update Trajectory-level Avoids variance amplification
Single-step episode (bandit) Either Choose based on implementation convenience
Need for compromise Segment-level Switch at natural boundaries

Core trade-off: Step/segment-level mixture is stronger on the sampling side (fast staleness removal), while trajectory-level mixture is more stable on the estimation side (easier surrogate objective estimation).

8. Handling Training-Inference Inconsistency

8.1 Background and Effective Staleness

In large-scale distributed training, policies on the inference side and training side may be inconsistent:

  • Numerical implementation differences: softmax normalization, quantization, kernel fusion
  • Decoding rule differences: temperature scaling, top-p/top-k sampling

Let the behavior policy modeled on the training side be ⟦INLMATH229MATHEND⟧, while the policy actually sampling on the inference side is ⟦INLMATH230MATHEND⟧.

Effective Staleness

Define effective staleness:

⟦DISPMATH28MATHEND⟧

This definition simultaneously covers version staleness and training-inference implementation differences.

8.2 Actionable Control

By Lemma 6.1, ⟦INLMATH231MATHEND⟧ can be written in a sample-computable form. Given threshold ⟦INLMATH232MATHEND⟧, if training only uses samples satisfying ⟦INLMATH233MATHEND⟧, then the effective staleness on the filtered training sub-distribution (which we may denote by ⟦INLMATH234MATHEND⟧) can be controlled to at most ⟦INLMATH235MATHEND⟧. This controls the filtered training distribution, not the original sampling distribution’s ⟦INLMATH236MATHEND⟧.

Key Implementation Points

  1. Behavior denominator alignment: The behavior probability in the loss should use the inference-side recorded ⟦INLMATH237MATHEND⟧
  2. Probability smoothing: If the inference side has truncation (e.g., top-k), ensure ratios are valid and the support-coverage condition required by Lemma 6.1 still holds

9. Practical Guidelines

Core Theoretical Framework

The structure of the monotonic improvement lower bound is:

⟦DISPMATH29MATHEND⟧

Here ⟦INLMATH238MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH239MATHEND⟧ are just compressed notation for the theoretical coefficients introduced earlier. Concretely, one can read them as

⟦DISPMATH30MATHEND⟧

up to the exact naming convention used in the intermediate statements. They are not free hyperparameters.

Separation of Concerns Principle

Control Term Responsible Party Control Mechanism Specific Operation
⟦INLMATH240MATHEND⟧ Optimization algorithm Policy clipping Clip update increments (e.g., clip ⟦INLMATH241MATHEND⟧)
⟦INLMATH242MATHEND⟧ Sampling system Data filtering Discard stale samples
⟦INLMATH243MATHEND⟧ Sampling system Version window Use only most recent ⟦INLMATH244MATHEND⟧ versions

Clipping Method Selection

Scenario Recommended Method Rationale
High staleness Method 1 (adaptive) Automatically tightens constraints for stale samples
Implementation simplicity prioritized Method 2 (incremental) Clipping form is simpler, though behavior logprobs are still needed for ⟦INLMATH245MATHEND⟧
LLM large vocabulary Method 1 Avoids slow updates for low-probability tokens

Handling Training-Inference Inconsistency

  • Use inference-side recorded ⟦INLMATH246MATHEND⟧ as the behavior denominator
  • Compress effective staleness through sample filtering

Appendix: Quick Reference for Key Symbols

Symbol Meaning
⟦INLMATH247MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH248MATHEND⟧ Latest policy at round ⟦INLMATH249MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH250MATHEND⟧-th old policy
⟦INLMATH251MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH252MATHEND⟧ Discounted state visitation distribution, advantage function
⟦INLMATH253MATHEND⟧ TV distance between two policies at state ⟦INLMATH254MATHEND⟧
⟦INLMATH255MATHEND⟧ Mixture behavior policy at round ⟦INLMATH256MATHEND⟧
⟦INLMATH257MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH258MATHEND⟧ Index transition kernel, initial index distribution
⟦INLMATH259MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH260MATHEND⟧ Update increment shift, sampling staleness
⟦INLMATH261MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH262MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH263MATHEND⟧ Clipping radius, staleness threshold, version window
⟦INLMATH264MATHEND⟧ Expected advantage upper bound constant

References

  1. John Schulman, Sergey Levine, Philipp Moritz, Michael I. Jordan, Pieter Abbeel. “Trust Region Policy Optimization” (TRPO). arXiv:1502.05477. https://arxiv.org/abs/1502.05477

  2. Joshua Achiam, David Held, Aviv Tamar, Pieter Abbeel. “Constrained Policy Optimization” (CPO). arXiv:1705.10528. https://arxiv.org/abs/1705.10528

  3. John Schulman, Filip Wolski, Prafulla Dhariwal, Alec Radford, Oleg Klimov. “Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms” (PPO). arXiv:1707.06347. https://arxiv.org/abs/1707.06347

  4. James Queeney, Ioannis Ch. Paschalidis, Christos G. Cassandras. “Generalized Proximal Policy Optimization with Sample Reuse” (GePPO). arXiv:2111.00072. https://arxiv.org/abs/2111.00072

  5. Yuzhen Zhou, Jiajun Li, Yusheng Su, et al. “APRIL: Active Partial Rollouts in Reinforcement Learning to Tame Long-tail Generation” (APRIL; partial rollout). arXiv:2509.18521. https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.18521

  6. Jacob Hilton, Karl Cobbe, John Schulman. “Batch size-invariance for policy optimization” (Decoupled PPO). arXiv:2110.00641. https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.00641

@misc{WangZhang2025OffPolicyLLMRL,
	author       = {Wang, Xihuai and Zhang, Shao},
	title        = {Taming Stale Data: Off-Policy Reinforcement Learning for LLMs with Monotonic Improvement Guarantees},
	year         = {2025},
	month        = dec,
	day          = {17},
	url          = {https://xihuai18.github.io/reinforcement-learning/2025/12/17/offpolicy-en.html},
	urldate      = {2025-12-17}
}
entry.summary: This post derives an off-policy view of LLM reinforcement learning: from single-policy performance bounds to multi-policy mixture sampling, with monotonic-improvement conditions, a decomposition via triangle inequality into update shift and sampling staleness, and practical clipping and filtering strategies.

entry.title: 驯服陈旧数据：LLM 强化学习的异策略训练与单调提升条件

link.title: 驯服陈旧数据：LLM 强化学习的异策略训练与单调提升条件

entry.content:
本文要回答一个更具体的问题：当训练批次混入多个历史策略版本的数据时，PPO 式更新在什么条件下还能保留一个可分析的单调提升下界？

1. 引言：为什么我们需要关心”异策略”？

用强化学习训练大语言模型时，最直接的做法是同策略（on-policy）训练：模型生成一批数据，立刻用这批数据更新，再用更新后的模型采样下一批。

但在大规模分布式训练中，数百个 GPU 并行采样，模型更新本身也有延迟。新版本发布时，旧版本生成的数据往往还留在队列里：直接丢掉太浪费，继续使用又担心数据已经过时。

这便是异策略（off-policy）训练所面临的核心问题：用旧策略采集的数据来更新新策略，究竟在什么条件下还能继续获得可靠的性能提升？

本文关心的就是这个问题：混合多个版本策略的数据来训练时，在什么条件下还能维持可分析的单调提升下界。

先说明一点：很多 LLM-RL 任务更接近单步 episode / contextual bandit。本文仍先在一般折扣 MDP 上推导，是为了把“多源采样、陈旧性、裁剪”这些结构写清楚；到第七部分再看 bandit 化之后哪些地方会明显简化。

你也可以把这篇文章看成前一篇“三策略视角”文章的延伸：这里只是把行为侧从单一 ⟦INLMATH30MATHEND⟧ 显式展开成历史策略混合 ⟦INLMATH31MATHEND⟧，而 ⟦INLMATH32MATHEND⟧ 与 ⟦INLMATH33MATHEND⟧ 则分别扮演当前参考策略与更新目标的角色。

2. 理论基础

2.1 基本设定

我们考虑一个标准的马尔可夫决策过程（MDP），包含状态空间 ⟦INLMATH34MATHEND⟧、动作空间 ⟦INLMATH35MATHEND⟧、转移概率 ⟦INLMATH36MATHEND⟧、奖励函数 ⟦INLMATH37MATHEND⟧、初始状态分布 ⟦INLMATH38MATHEND⟧ 和折扣因子 ⟦INLMATH39MATHEND⟧。

策略 ⟦INLMATH40MATHEND⟧ 的期望累计折扣回报为：

⟦DISPMATH9MATHEND⟧

折扣状态访问分布

定义为策略长期运行中访问各状态的加权频率：

⟦DISPMATH10MATHEND⟧

优势函数

用于衡量在状态 ⟦INLMATH41MATHEND⟧ 下选择动作 ⟦INLMATH42MATHEND⟧ 相对于策略平均水平的优劣：

⟦DISPMATH11MATHEND⟧

全变差距离（TV 距离）

用于衡量两个策略在给定状态 ⟦INLMATH43MATHEND⟧ 下动作分布的差异：

⟦DISPMATH12MATHEND⟧

本文统一用 ⟦INLMATH44MATHEND⟧ 表示条件概率（例如 ⟦INLMATH45MATHEND⟧），并保留 ⟦INLMATH46MATHEND⟧ 表示范数。

2.2 核心工具：性能差分引理

整个理论的基石是如下简洁的结论：

引理 2.1（性能差分引理）

对于任意旧策略 ⟦INLMATH47MATHEND⟧ 和新策略 ⟦INLMATH48MATHEND⟧，性能差异可表示为：

⟦DISPMATH1MATHEND⟧

直观理解：新策略带来的改进，等于它自身访问到的状态分布下，按它选动作所得到的平均优势。

3. 单策略采样的性能改进下界

3.1 分布不匹配与分布差异控制

性能差分引理存在一个实际问题：右侧的期望是在新策略的状态分布 ⟦INLMATH49MATHEND⟧ 下计算的，而我们只能从旧策略的分布 ⟦INLMATH50MATHEND⟧ 中采样。

解决思路是：将期望分解为“旧分布下的期望”与“偏差项”两部分，然后对偏差项加以控制。关键问题在于：状态分布的差异与策略的差异之间存在怎样的定量关系？

状态分布差异的控制

引理 3.1（状态分布差异与策略TV距离的关系）

⟦DISPMATH2MATHEND⟧

这里直接采用的是 average-divergence / average-TV 风格的写法。它不是 TRPO 中最常见的 ⟦INLMATH51MATHEND⟧ 版本，而更接近 CPO / Achiam et al. (2017) 那类“用平均散度刻画性能差距”的表述；这样写更容易落到样本平均，也更方便后文处理多源采样与陈旧性。

物理意义

策略在动作空间上的微小差异，会通过环境动力学被“放大”为状态访问分布的差异。上式中的系数 ⟦INLMATH52MATHEND⟧ 反映了时间累积效应——在长时域任务中（⟦INLMATH53MATHEND⟧ 接近1），放大效应更为显著。

证明思路

证明通常从折扣访问分布的不动点方程出发，再配合 average-divergence 风格的性能界来完成。由于本文重点不在复现这条证明链，而在于如何把它推广到多源采样与陈旧数据场景，故此处不再展开完整推导。

3.2 策略性能改进下界

定理 3.2（策略性能改进下界）

定义期望优势上界常数 ⟦INLMATH54MATHEND⟧，则：

⟦DISPMATH3MATHEND⟧

其中代理目标为：

⟦DISPMATH4MATHEND⟧

这里的 ⟦INLMATH55MATHEND⟧ 省略了与 ⟦INLMATH56MATHEND⟧ 无关的常数项 ⟦INLMATH57MATHEND⟧；若写成更接近 TRPO 教科书的形式，就是 ⟦INLMATH58MATHEND⟧。另外，⟦INLMATH59MATHEND⟧ 本身依赖新策略 ⟦INLMATH60MATHEND⟧，所以它更像一个出现在下界表达式中的结构性系数，而不是实践里可以直接当作固定超参数的常数。

这个下界由两部分组成：

  1. 代理目标 ⟦INLMATH61MATHEND⟧：可通过旧策略数据利用重要性采样直接估计，它是 TRPO 的经典 surrogate，也是 PPO clipped / penalized 目标的出发点。

  2. 策略偏移惩罚：随着新旧策略的TV距离增大而增加，这解释了为何PPO等算法需要限制更新幅度。

核心结论：该定理给出了一个显式的改进下界；当右侧为正时，就能保证性能改进。

4. 多策略静态混合采样

4.1 问题设定与统一建模（静态混合）

在实际训练中，一个批次的数据可能来自多个策略版本 ⟦INLMATH62MATHEND⟧，各版本占比为 ⟦INLMATH63MATHEND⟧。如何将定理 3.2 推广到这种情形？

核心思想：扩展状态空间

做法很直接：把策略版本索引并入状态空间。

定义扩展状态空间 ⟦INLMATH64MATHEND⟧，其中 ⟦INLMATH65MATHEND⟧ 是策略索引集合。在扩展状态 ⟦INLMATH66MATHEND⟧ 下，混合行为策略定义为 ⟦INLMATH67MATHEND⟧。

索引的演化由索引转移核 ⟦INLMATH68MATHEND⟧ 刻画。扩展MDP继承原始MDP的奖励和环境转移，索引按 ⟦INLMATH69MATHEND⟧ 独立演化。

这个技巧之所以有效，是因为新策略 ⟦INLMATH70MATHEND⟧ 在扩展MDP上的回报与在原始MDP中的回报相同，从而可以直接应用定理 3.2。

4.2 轨迹级混合：结构简化与改进下界

最常见的情形是每条轨迹仅使用一个旧策略：在轨迹开始时采样索引 ⟦INLMATH71MATHEND⟧，随后整条轨迹都使用策略 ⟦INLMATH72MATHEND⟧。此时索引转移核为恒等转移：⟦INLMATH73MATHEND⟧。

从工程实现的角度看，在许多 actor-learner 的异步训练架构中（如果采样端与训练端将数据按“整条轨迹/完整 episode 归属于某个策略版本”的方式组织），这可以近似对应这里的轨迹级混合：actor 在一个采样单元内固定使用某个策略快照生成数据，learner 再混合使用来自不同版本的整条轨迹数据进行更新。这里使用“近似”一词，是因为不同系统对“轨迹/采样单元”的切分边界可能不完全一致。

引理 4.1（轨迹级混合的结构简化）

(a) 扩展状态访问分布分解为：⟦INLMATH74MATHEND⟧

(b) 优势函数还原为：⟦INLMATH75MATHEND⟧

(b) 的直观理解：由于索引永不改变，从扩展状态 ⟦INLMATH76MATHEND⟧ 出发的所有未来轨迹都由同一个策略 ⟦INLMATH77MATHEND⟧ 生成。因此，未来的累计回报完全由 ⟦INLMATH78MATHEND⟧ 决定，价值函数和优势函数自然还原为 ⟦INLMATH79MATHEND⟧ 的对应量。

因此，混合策略的回报等于各旧策略回报的加权平均：⟦INLMATH80MATHEND⟧。

改进下界

推论 4.2（轨迹级混合的性能改进下界）

⟦DISPMATH5MATHEND⟧

这里取 ⟦INLMATH81MATHEND⟧ 只是为了把表达写得更紧凑；更细的写法是让每个分量各自带自己的 ⟦INLMATH82MATHEND⟧ 再加权求和。也就是说，这里额外牺牲了一些紧度，换来更统一的展示形式。

这说明：只要对每条轨迹用对应旧策略的重要性比率构造损失，并控制新策略与各旧策略之间的偏移，混合训练仍然有明确的改进下界。

5. 动态混合采样与单调提升条件

5.1 问题与统一建模（动态混合）

上一部分讨论的是静态混合——混合权重 ⟦INLMATH83MATHEND⟧ 固定不变。本节考虑更一般的动态混合——即新策略发布后，采样逐步由新策略接管的过程。

前面的结论刻画了“新策略相对于混合行为策略”的改进。但在实际训练中，我们真正关心的是：每轮更新后的最新策略 ⟦INLMATH84MATHEND⟧ 相对于上一轮最新策略 ⟦INLMATH85MATHEND⟧ 是否具有单调提升性？

⟦DISPMATH13MATHEND⟧

统一建模框架

动态混合采样的两种典型形式都可以用索引转移核 ⟦INLMATH86MATHEND⟧ 统一刻画：

轨迹级混合（可类比为常规异步训练的一个抽象；索引恒等转移）：⟦INLMATH87MATHEND⟧

步/段级混合（partial rollout / 段式采样的一个抽象；允许切换）：⟦INLMATH88MATHEND⟧

其中 ⟦INLMATH89MATHEND⟧ 为切换概率，⟦INLMATH90MATHEND⟧ 为目标索引分布。

5.2 分解与单调提升下界

通过引入混合回报 ⟦INLMATH91MATHEND⟧ 作为中间桥梁，性能差异可分解为：

⟦DISPMATH14MATHEND⟧

第一项可用定理 3.2 处理。第二项是混合偏差项，它可以通过把 ⟦INLMATH92MATHEND⟧ 分解成各个 ⟦INLMATH93MATHEND⟧ 的加权和，再对各分量应用 TV 上界来得到。这里先直接给出结果：

⟦DISPMATH15MATHEND⟧

单调提升下界

合并上述结果，我们得到核心定理：

定理 5.1（动态混合采样下的单调提升下界）

⟦DISPMATH6MATHEND⟧

其中 ⟦INLMATH94MATHEND⟧ 表示“相对行为策略 ⟦INLMATH95MATHEND⟧ 的代理目标”（与第三部分的 ⟦INLMATH96MATHEND⟧ 同形，只是把行为策略从单一 ⟦INLMATH97MATHEND⟧ 推广到混合 ⟦INLMATH98MATHEND⟧）。

更具体地，可写为

⟦DISPMATH16MATHEND⟧

类似地，记

⟦DISPMATH17MATHEND⟧

这个下界里有两个惩罚项，对应双重控制：

  • 更新偏移惩罚：新策略 ⟦INLMATH99MATHEND⟧ 相对于采样来源策略 ⟦INLMATH100MATHEND⟧ 的偏移
  • 采样陈旧性惩罚：采样来源策略 ⟦INLMATH101MATHEND⟧ 相对于当前策略 ⟦INLMATH102MATHEND⟧ 的陈旧性

5.3 直接约束为何不可行：三角不等式分解

定理 5.1 中的更新偏移惩罚项看似可以通过约束 ⟦INLMATH103MATHEND⟧ 来控制；但如果把它理解成“对每个采样来源策略都施加统一的 trust-region 约束”，就会遇到一个实际上的不可行性问题：

观察 5.2（更新偏移约束的不可行性）

假设混合采样包含两个旧策略 ⟦INLMATH104MATHEND⟧ 和 ⟦INLMATH105MATHEND⟧，若存在某个状态 ⟦INLMATH106MATHEND⟧ 使得 ⟦INLMATH107MATHEND⟧，则不存在任何策略 ⟦INLMATH108MATHEND⟧ 能够同时满足 ⟦INLMATH109MATHEND⟧ 与 ⟦INLMATH110MATHEND⟧。

证明

由三角不等式，若同时满足两个约束，则 ⟦INLMATH111MATHEND⟧，矛盾。

问题根源

更新偏移惩罚项将 ⟦INLMATH112MATHEND⟧ 与历史策略族 ⟦INLMATH113MATHEND⟧ 直接耦合，而后者的内部结构是历史训练的产物，不受当前更新控制。

三角不等式分解

解决方案是利用TV距离的三角不等式：

⟦DISPMATH18MATHEND⟧

这将耦合约束拆分为两个独立部分：

  • 更新增量偏移 ⟦INLMATH114MATHEND⟧：新策略相对于当前策略的偏离，可由优化侧控制
  • 采样陈旧性 ⟦INLMATH115MATHEND⟧：当前策略相对于各旧策略的偏离，需由采样侧控制

定义：

⟦DISPMATH19MATHEND⟧

推论 5.3（分解后的单调提升下界）

⟦DISPMATH7MATHEND⟧

为何分解能解决问题？

关键在于：分解后的 ⟦INLMATH116MATHEND⟧ 只涉及新策略 ⟦INLMATH117MATHEND⟧ 和当前策略 ⟦INLMATH118MATHEND⟧，与旧策略族 ⟦INLMATH119MATHEND⟧ 的结构完全无关。因此，无论旧策略之间差异多大，约束 ⟦INLMATH120MATHEND⟧ 都是可行的——这正是观察 5.2 所揭示的不可行性问题的解决之道。

对应的工程原则就是职责分离：

控制项 负责方 控制手段
⟦INLMATH121MATHEND⟧（更新增量偏移） 优化算法 策略裁剪
⟦INLMATH122MATHEND⟧（采样陈旧性） 采样系统 数据过滤、版本窗口

6. 裁剪机制的理论基础

6.1 从 TV 距离到样本可控量

推论 5.3 告诉我们，要保证单调提升，需要控制更新增量偏移 ⟦INLMATH123MATHEND⟧。但TV距离是分布层面的量，如何用样本来控制它？

连接理论和样本的是下面这个恒等式：

引理 6.1（TV距离的比值差表示）

设策略 ⟦INLMATH124MATHEND⟧ 的支撑覆盖 ⟦INLMATH125MATHEND⟧ 和 ⟦INLMATH126MATHEND⟧ 的支撑，则对任意状态分布 ⟦INLMATH127MATHEND⟧：

⟦DISPMATH8MATHEND⟧

直观理解

左侧是两个分布之间的TV距离（需要遍历所有动作），右侧是在 ⟦INLMATH128MATHEND⟧ 下采样时两个重要性比值的差的绝对值。这使我们能够通过样本来估计和控制TV距离。

⟦INLMATH129MATHEND⟧ 的样本表示

利用引理 6.1，取 ⟦INLMATH130MATHEND⟧，⟦INLMATH131MATHEND⟧，⟦INLMATH132MATHEND⟧（采样来源策略），可得：

⟦DISPMATH20MATHEND⟧

记 ⟦INLMATH133MATHEND⟧ 和 ⟦INLMATH134MATHEND⟧，则：

⟦DISPMATH21MATHEND⟧

这意味着：如果我们在理论上硬性要求每个样本都满足 ⟦INLMATH135MATHEND⟧，就能保证 ⟦INLMATH136MATHEND⟧。

6.2 约束 ⟦INLMATH137MATHEND⟧：两种裁剪方式

方法一：直接约束比值差

对每个样本 ⟦INLMATH138MATHEND⟧，要求满足：

⟦DISPMATH22MATHEND⟧

即裁剪区间为 ⟦INLMATH139MATHEND⟧，裁剪中心是 ⟦INLMATH140MATHEND⟧ 而非 1。

方法二：约束增量比值

注意到 ⟦INLMATH141MATHEND⟧，因此有：

⟦DISPMATH23MATHEND⟧

如果进一步在理论上硬性约束 ⟦INLMATH142MATHEND⟧，由于 ⟦INLMATH143MATHEND⟧，可以证明 ⟦INLMATH144MATHEND⟧。

这种方法直接对 ⟦INLMATH145MATHEND⟧ 以 1 为中心进行裁剪，裁剪约束本身不依赖旧策略 ⟦INLMATH146MATHEND⟧。但如果采用后文的 ⟦INLMATH147MATHEND⟧，仍需要每条样本的行为概率 ⟦INLMATH148MATHEND⟧（或记录的 logprob）来计算 ⟦INLMATH149MATHEND⟧。

先强调一点：上面两条都是理论上的逐样本硬约束。下面写出的 clipped surrogate 只是实践中的近似实现，目的是把 ⟦INLMATH150MATHEND⟧ 控制在一个可接受的范围内，而不是让每一步优化都自动满足严格保证。设当前样本来自旧策略 ⟦INLMATH151MATHEND⟧，记：

  • ⟦INLMATH152MATHEND⟧（新策略相对采样策略的比值）
  • ⟦INLMATH153MATHEND⟧（当前策略相对采样策略的比值）
  • ⟦INLMATH154MATHEND⟧（新策略相对当前策略的增量比值）

说明：若采用轨迹级混合（索引不变），则 ⟦INLMATH155MATHEND⟧，可直接用每条轨迹对应旧策略的优势估计；若为步/段级混合，直接用 ⟦INLMATH156MATHEND⟧ 代替 ⟦INLMATH157MATHEND⟧ 会引入优势替代偏差（第七部分详述），需要使用能反映未来索引切换的优势/价值估计。

标准 PPO（轨迹级混合时）

以 1 为中心裁剪 ⟦INLMATH158MATHEND⟧

⟦DISPMATH24MATHEND⟧

方法一

以 ⟦INLMATH159MATHEND⟧ 为中心裁剪 ⟦INLMATH160MATHEND⟧

⟦DISPMATH25MATHEND⟧

方法二

以 1 为中心裁剪增量比值 ⟦INLMATH161MATHEND⟧

⟦DISPMATH26MATHEND⟧

其中 ⟦INLMATH162MATHEND⟧ 是经过重要性加权的优势估计。

6.3 对比与落地：选型与采样侧控制

表 6.1　三种裁剪机制的对比

方法 裁剪变量 裁剪中心 裁剪区间 主要对应的目标 TV 距离
标准PPO ⟦INLMATH163MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH164MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH165MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH166MATHEND⟧
方法一 ⟦INLMATH167MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH168MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH169MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH170MATHEND⟧
方法二 ⟦INLMATH171MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH172MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH173MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH174MATHEND⟧

标准 PPO 的根本问题（多策略混合）

如果沿用单源 PPO 的 trust-region 解读，标准 PPO 约束的是 ⟦INLMATH175MATHEND⟧，也就是要求新策略同时接近所有采样来源策略。根据观察 5.2，当各旧策略 ⟦INLMATH176MATHEND⟧ 之间差异显著时，这组约束可能无法同时满足。结果就是信赖域交集收缩甚至接近为空，更新容易被最陈旧的策略所限制。

方法一与方法二的共同优势

两者都约束 ⟦INLMATH177MATHEND⟧——新策略相对于当前策略（而非采样策略）的偏离。由于 ⟦INLMATH178MATHEND⟧ 是唯一确定的，这个约束对所有来源的样本一致，完全规避了不可行性问题。

方法一 vs 方法二

比较维度 方法一（自适应裁剪） 方法二（增量裁剪）
陈旧样本（⟦INLMATH179MATHEND⟧） 自动收紧约束，更保守 可能产生大梯度方差
LLM大词表低概率token 允许较大绝对变化（加法型） 绝对变化受限（乘法型）
实现复杂度 需存储 ⟦INLMATH180MATHEND⟧ 和 ⟦INLMATH181MATHEND⟧ 需 ⟦INLMATH182MATHEND⟧ 与 ⟦INLMATH183MATHEND⟧（或存储的 logprob）以计算 ⟦INLMATH184MATHEND⟧；裁剪本身仅用 ⟦INLMATH185MATHEND⟧
优势函数 使用 ⟦INLMATH186MATHEND⟧ 使用加权优势 ⟦INLMATH187MATHEND⟧

详细解释

维度一：陈旧样本处理

当样本来自很旧的策略时，⟦INLMATH188MATHEND⟧ 可能很大。

  • 方法二的被积函数为 ⟦INLMATH189MATHEND⟧，即便 ⟦INLMATH190MATHEND⟧，被积函数仍可达 ⟦INLMATH191MATHEND⟧，产生尖峰。
  • 方法一直接约束 ⟦INLMATH192MATHEND⟧，被积函数上界恒为 ⟦INLMATH193MATHEND⟧，不受 ⟦INLMATH194MATHEND⟧ 放大。

维度二：LLM 大词表问题

大语言模型词表规模巨大，大量token的概率极小。

  • 方法二约束 ⟦INLMATH195MATHEND⟧，这是乘法型约束：若 ⟦INLMATH196MATHEND⟧，允许的绝对变化仅为 ⟦INLMATH197MATHEND⟧。
  • 方法一约束 ⟦INLMATH198MATHEND⟧，这是尺度由采样策略概率 ⟦INLMATH199MATHEND⟧ 决定的约束：若该 token 在旧策略下概率较高（例如 ⟦INLMATH200MATHEND⟧），即便当前概率很低，也允许较快提升；当然，这仍以该 token 在采样分布中有足够可观测质量为前提。

采样陈旧性的控制

推论 5.3 表明，⟦INLMATH201MATHEND⟧ 同样影响单调提升下界，但它无法通过优化侧的裁剪来控制，需要由采样系统实现：

(一) 丢弃陈旧数据

设定阈值 ⟦INLMATH202MATHEND⟧，对每个样本计算 ⟦INLMATH203MATHEND⟧，丢弃超过该阈值的样本。

(二) 控制策略版本窗口

限制混合采样的旧策略版本数量，例如仅使用最近 ⟦INLMATH204MATHEND⟧ 个版本的数据。

裁剪的操作含义

最后，需要澄清裁剪与理论下界的关系。

推论 5.3 中，⟦INLMATH205MATHEND⟧ 的系数 ⟦INLMATH206MATHEND⟧ 依赖于新策略 ⟦INLMATH207MATHEND⟧，因此惩罚项不能简单地替换为常数。正确的操作含义是：

在 ⟦INLMATH208MATHEND⟧ 的约束下，最大化代理目标 ⟦INLMATH209MATHEND⟧

裁剪目标函数可以被理解为这一约束优化的近似实现——通过裁剪近似限制更新幅度，促使 ⟦INLMATH210MATHEND⟧ 保持可控；在此前提下，再通过梯度上升提升代理目标，从而尽量贴近前述单调改进分析。

本节小结

本节建立了裁剪机制的理论基础：

  1. 引理 6.1将TV距离转化为样本层面的比值差，是连接理论与实现的桥梁
  2. 两种约束方法：方法一（自适应裁剪中心）和方法二（固定增量裁剪）的硬约束版本都能保证 ⟦INLMATH211MATHEND⟧；实践中的 clipped surrogate 则是对这一思路的近似实现
  3. 与标准PPO对比：若沿用单源 trust-region 的解释，标准PPO约束 ⟦INLMATH212MATHEND⟧，在多策略混合下会遇到约束难以同时满足的问题；方法一/二把目标改为 ⟦INLMATH213MATHEND⟧，从而规避这一结构性困难
  4. 方法选择：陈旧性高或LLM大词表场景推荐方法一；若更关注“裁剪/信赖域不再依赖旧策略族”，可选方法二（但仍需数据侧提供行为 logprob 以计算 ⟦INLMATH214MATHEND⟧）
  5. ⟦INLMATH215MATHEND⟧ 的控制由采样侧负责，通过数据过滤和版本窗口实现
  6. 裁剪是约束优化：在 ⟦INLMATH216MATHEND⟧ 约束下最大化代理目标

7. 轨迹级与步/段级混合的比较

7.1 机制差异与估计影响

两类混合机制的本质区别在于索引转移核的结构：

  • 轨迹级混合：⟦INLMATH217MATHEND⟧，索引永不改变
  • 步/段级混合：⟦INLMATH218MATHEND⟧，允许轨迹内切换

与常见工程术语的对应关系如下：

  • 这里的轨迹级混合可以大致理解为常规异步训练的一个理想化抽象：数据按整条轨迹/episode 归属于某个策略版本；
  • 这里的步/段级混合可以大致理解为partial rollout的一个抽象：由于 actor 与 learner 异步，且 segment 边界处可能刷新到新策略版本，使用索引转移核允许“轨迹内部版本切换”，可以更好地近似刻画这种现象。

关键分水岭在于引理 4.1 的结构简化是否成立：轨迹级混合满足优势函数还原；步/段级混合一般不满足，因为未来回报受索引转移核影响。

采样陈旧性 ⟦INLMATH219MATHEND⟧ 的差异

轨迹级混合的陈旧性来源于：混合权重 ⟦INLMATH220MATHEND⟧ 在新策略发布后仍对旧策略保留一定的比例。

步/段级混合具有指数压缩效应：考虑从旧到新以概率 ⟦INLMATH221MATHEND⟧ 切换的简化模型，折扣访问分布下旧索引的边缘质量为 ⟦INLMATH222MATHEND⟧。只要 ⟦INLMATH223MATHEND⟧，旧策略的权重即可被显著压缩。

代理目标估计的差异

轨迹级混合：优势函数还原为 ⟦INLMATH224MATHEND⟧，估计路径清晰。

步/段级混合的优势替代偏差：若沿用单策略优势估计，将产生系统性偏差。原因是 ⟦INLMATH225MATHEND⟧ 需要对未来索引切换取期望，而 ⟦INLMATH226MATHEND⟧ 隐含了“未来始终沿用 ⟦INLMATH227MATHEND⟧”的假设。

Bandit 设定下的统一

在单步 episode 的 LLM 训练中，无后续状态转移，两类机制的估计问题统一，无上述偏差。

7.2 风险与适用场景

步/段级混合还有一个隐患：即便单步重要性比值被裁剪，长轨迹下多步噪声叠加仍会放大梯度估计方差。当每次更新的策略变化幅度较大时，轨迹内部的“行为突变”可能引发更重尾的比值分布。这也是表6.1中“策略变化幅度大”场景推荐轨迹级混合的原因。

适用场景

表 7.1　两类混合机制的适用场景

场景特征 推荐机制 理由
长轨迹、高频更新、强异步 步/段级 可显著压缩 ⟦INLMATH228MATHEND⟧
短轨迹（非Bandit） 轨迹级 ⟦INLMATH229MATHEND⟧ 自然较低
每次更新策略变化幅度大 轨迹级 避免方差放大
单步episode（Bandit） 均可 按实现便利选择
需要折中方案 段级 在自然边界切换

核心权衡：步/段级混合在采样侧更强（快速去陈旧），轨迹级混合在估计侧更稳（代理目标易于估计）。

8. 训推不一致的处理

8.1 背景与有效陈旧性

在大规模分布式训练中，推理端和训练端的策略可能存在不一致：

  • 数值实现差异：softmax归一化、量化、核融合等
  • 解码规则差异：温度缩放、top-p/top-k采样等

设训练侧建模的行为策略为 ⟦INLMATH230MATHEND⟧，而推理端实际采样的策略为 ⟦INLMATH231MATHEND⟧。

有效陈旧性

定义有效陈旧性：

⟦DISPMATH27MATHEND⟧

该定义同时覆盖了版本陈旧性与训推实现差异。

8.2 可操作控制

由引理 6.1，⟦INLMATH232MATHEND⟧ 可表示为样本级可计算形式。给定阈值 ⟦INLMATH233MATHEND⟧，若训练仅使用满足 ⟦INLMATH234MATHEND⟧ 的样本，则参与训练的数据子分布上的有效陈旧性（可记为 ⟦INLMATH235MATHEND⟧）可被控制在 ⟦INLMATH236MATHEND⟧ 以内。换言之，这里控制的是过滤后的训练分布，而不是原始采样分布上的 ⟦INLMATH237MATHEND⟧。

关键实现要点

  1. 行为分母对齐：损失中的行为概率应使用推理端记录的 ⟦INLMATH238MATHEND⟧
  2. 概率平滑：若推理端有截断（如 top-k），需通过平滑等方式确保比值合法，并满足引理 6.1 所需的支撑覆盖条件

9. 总结：实践指南

核心理论框架

单调提升下界的结构为：

⟦DISPMATH28MATHEND⟧

这里的 ⟦INLMATH239MATHEND⟧ 只是对前文理论常数的压缩记号，用来概括下界结构。更具体地，可把它们理解为

⟦DISPMATH29MATHEND⟧

只是在正文里为了突出结构，把记号压缩成了 ⟦INLMATH240MATHEND⟧。它们不是实践里可自由调节的超参数。

职责分离原则

控制项 负责方 控制手段 具体操作
⟦INLMATH241MATHEND⟧ 优化算法 策略裁剪 对更新增量进行裁剪（例如对 ⟦INLMATH242MATHEND⟧ 裁剪）
⟦INLMATH243MATHEND⟧ 采样系统 数据过滤 丢弃陈旧样本
⟦INLMATH244MATHEND⟧ 采样系统 版本窗口 仅用最近 ⟦INLMATH245MATHEND⟧ 个版本

裁剪方法选择

场景 推荐方法 理由
陈旧性较高 方法一（自适应） 自动对陈旧样本收紧约束
实现简洁优先 方法二（增量） 裁剪形式更简洁，但仍需行为 logprob / ⟦INLMATH246MATHEND⟧ 参与优势构造
LLM大词表 方法一 避免低概率token更新过慢

训推不一致的处理

  • 使用推理端记录的 ⟦INLMATH247MATHEND⟧ 作为行为分母
  • 通过样本过滤压缩参与训练数据上的有效陈旧性

附录：关键符号速查表

符号 含义
⟦INLMATH248MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH249MATHEND⟧ 第 ⟦INLMATH250MATHEND⟧ 轮最新策略，第 ⟦INLMATH251MATHEND⟧ 个旧策略
⟦INLMATH252MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH253MATHEND⟧ 折扣状态访问分布，优势函数
⟦INLMATH254MATHEND⟧ 两策略在状态 ⟦INLMATH255MATHEND⟧ 上的TV距离
⟦INLMATH256MATHEND⟧ 第 ⟦INLMATH257MATHEND⟧ 轮混合行为策略
⟦INLMATH258MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH259MATHEND⟧ 索引转移核，索引初始分布
⟦INLMATH260MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH261MATHEND⟧ 更新增量偏移，采样陈旧性
⟦INLMATH262MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH263MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH264MATHEND⟧ 裁剪半径，陈旧性阈值，版本窗口
⟦INLMATH265MATHEND⟧ 期望优势上界常数

参考文献

  1. John Schulman, Sergey Levine, Philipp Moritz, Michael I. Jordan, Pieter Abbeel. “Trust Region Policy Optimization” (TRPO). arXiv:1502.05477. https://arxiv.org/abs/1502.05477

  2. Joshua Achiam, David Held, Aviv Tamar, Pieter Abbeel. “Constrained Policy Optimization” (CPO). arXiv:1705.10528. https://arxiv.org/abs/1705.10528

  3. John Schulman, Filip Wolski, Prafulla Dhariwal, Alec Radford, Oleg Klimov. “Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms” (PPO). arXiv:1707.06347. https://arxiv.org/abs/1707.06347

  4. James Queeney, Ioannis Ch. Paschalidis, Christos G. Cassandras. “Generalized Proximal Policy Optimization with Sample Reuse” (GePPO). arXiv:2111.00072. https://arxiv.org/abs/2111.00072

  5. Yuzhen Zhou, Jiajun Li, Yusheng Su, et al. “APRIL: Active Partial Rollouts in Reinforcement Learning to Tame Long-tail Generation” (APRIL; partial rollout). arXiv:2509.18521. https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.18521

  6. Jacob Hilton, Karl Cobbe, John Schulman. “Batch size-invariance for policy optimization” (Decoupled PPO). arXiv:2110.00641. https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.00641

@misc{WangZhang2025OffPolicyLLMRL,
	author       = {Wang, Xihuai and Zhang, Shao},
	title        = {驯服陈旧数据：LLM 强化学习的异策略训练与单调提升条件},
	year         = {2025},
	month        = dec,

entry.summary: 本文从单策略采样的性能改进下界出发，推导 LLM 强化学习中的异策略训练问题，扩展到多策略静态/动态混合采样，并把单调提升条件拆成更新增量偏移与采样陈旧性两部分，最后落到可操作的裁剪与过滤策略。

entry.title: Understanding KL Divergence Estimators in RL: From Numerical Estimation to Gradient Estimation

link.title: Understanding KL Divergence Estimators in RL: From Numerical Estimation to Gradient Estimation

entry.content:
Different KL estimators can behave quite differently in RL training. This post compares three estimators ⟦INLMATH71MATHEND⟧ in both on-policy and off-policy settings, and shows how the answer changes depending on whether KL is used as a differentiable loss term or as a detached reward-shaping term.

1. Introduction: The Role of KL Divergence in Reinforcement Learning

In policy optimization (PPO, GRPO, etc.) and alignment training (RLHF/RLAIF), a KL penalty keeps the updated policy from drifting too far from a reference policy. But “adding a KL penalty” hides several separate design choices: which estimator (⟦INLMATH72MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH73MATHEND⟧, ⟦INLMATH74MATHEND⟧), which sampling distribution (on-policy vs. off-policy), and how the KL term enters optimization (as a differentiable loss term vs. as a detached reward-shaping term). This post makes those choices explicit and clarifies how they interact.

1.1 The Distinction Between Forward KL and Reverse KL

Let ⟦INLMATH75MATHEND⟧ be the current actor policy, ⟦INLMATH76MATHEND⟧ the reference policy. The two directions of KL divergence are:

Reverse KL:

⟦DISPMATH1MATHEND⟧

Forward KL:

⟦DISPMATH2MATHEND⟧

Intuition:

  • Reverse KL is mode-seeking: the policy concentrates on high-probability regions of ⟦INLMATH77MATHEND⟧, possibly sacrificing diversity.
  • Forward KL is mass-covering: the policy tries to cover the support of ⟦INLMATH78MATHEND⟧.

RLHF typically uses reverse KL because we want the actor not to stray too far from the reference, rather than requiring it to cover every mode.

1.2 The Three Core Questions: Who to Sample From, What to Estimate, How to Use

When implementing a KL penalty, it helps to separate three interrelated questions:

  1. Who to sample from? Do samples come from the current policy ⟦INLMATH79MATHEND⟧ (on-policy), or from a behavior policy ⟦INLMATH80MATHEND⟧ (off-policy)?
  2. What to estimate? Are we trying to estimate reverse KL ⟦INLMATH81MATHEND⟧ or forward KL ⟦INLMATH82MATHEND⟧?
  3. How to use it? Is the KL term used as a differentiable loss term, or as a detached reward penalty (stop-gradient)?

Different combinations of these three questions point to different estimators. The goal of this post is simply to make those choices explicit and usable.

3. Three Estimators: Definitions and Design Principles

Let ⟦INLMATH128MATHEND⟧ denote the ratio. John Schulman proposed three single-sample estimators, defined as follows:

3.1 The Three Estimators: Definitions and Intuition

⟦INLMATH129MATHEND⟧: The Naive Log-Ratio Estimator

⟦DISPMATH12MATHEND⟧

This is the most direct definition: the negative log-ratio. It is unbiased for reverse KL, but it has a major drawback: it can be negative, while KL divergence is always non-negative. This can lead to high variance because positive and negative samples cancel.

⟦INLMATH130MATHEND⟧: The Squared Log-Ratio Estimator

⟦DISPMATH13MATHEND⟧

Design motivation: ⟦INLMATH131MATHEND⟧ can be either positive or negative; squaring yields an estimator where every sample is non-negative, and each sample measures the magnitude of mismatch between ⟦INLMATH132MATHEND⟧ and ⟦INLMATH133MATHEND⟧.

Why is the bias often small? More precisely, ⟦INLMATH134MATHEND⟧ is not a standard f-divergence. The safer statement is local: near a point ⟦INLMATH135MATHEND⟧ where ⟦INLMATH136MATHEND⟧, it shares the same second-order behavior as reverse KL. Under the usual regularity conditions that justify exchanging differentiation and integration, a small perturbation ⟦INLMATH137MATHEND⟧ gives

⟦DISPMATH14MATHEND⟧

and at the same time

⟦DISPMATH15MATHEND⟧

Here ⟦INLMATH138MATHEND⟧ is the Fisher information matrix at ⟦INLMATH139MATHEND⟧. So when the two policies are close, ⟦INLMATH140MATHEND⟧ and the true reverse KL share the same local curvature, and the discrepancy only shows up in higher-order terms. Once you leave that small-KL neighborhood, the bias is no longer guaranteed to stay small.

⟦INLMATH141MATHEND⟧: The Bregman Divergence Estimator via Control Variates

⟦DISPMATH16MATHEND⟧

Design motivation: We want an estimator that is both unbiased and low variance. A standard approach is to add a control variate to ⟦INLMATH142MATHEND⟧—a term with zero expectation that (ideally) is negatively correlated with ⟦INLMATH143MATHEND⟧.

Note that ⟦INLMATH144MATHEND⟧, so for any ⟦INLMATH145MATHEND⟧,

⟦DISPMATH17MATHEND⟧

remains an unbiased estimator.

Why choose ⟦INLMATH146MATHEND⟧? Since ⟦INLMATH147MATHEND⟧ is concave, we have ⟦INLMATH148MATHEND⟧, therefore

⟦DISPMATH18MATHEND⟧

It is always non-negative. This ensures every sample contributes “positively” to the estimate, eliminating the cancellation problem of ⟦INLMATH149MATHEND⟧.

Geometric intuition: ⟦INLMATH150MATHEND⟧ is actually a Bregman divergence. Consider the convex function ⟦INLMATH151MATHEND⟧, whose tangent at ⟦INLMATH152MATHEND⟧ is ⟦INLMATH153MATHEND⟧. The Bregman divergence is defined as the difference between the function value and the tangent value:

⟦DISPMATH19MATHEND⟧

Since a convex function always lies above its tangent, this gap is naturally non-negative. More importantly, as ⟦INLMATH154MATHEND⟧, the gap shrinks at a second-order rate ⟦INLMATH155MATHEND⟧, which is the fundamental reason why ⟦INLMATH156MATHEND⟧ tends to have lower variance when the policies are close.

Estimator Definition Design Principle
⟦INLMATH157MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH158MATHEND⟧ Naive definition
⟦INLMATH159MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH160MATHEND⟧ Local second-order KL surrogate
⟦INLMATH161MATHEND⟧ ⟦INLMATH162MATHEND⟧ Control variate + Bregman divergence

With the definitions and design principles in place, we first analyze their behavior as value estimators of KL—specifically, bias and variance.

This section analyzes the properties of the three estimators when estimating KL values. These properties are fundamental in any usage scenario.

From a pure value-estimation perspective, ⟦INLMATH187MATHEND⟧ is often the best choice among the unbiased estimators in the common small-KL, well-covered regime because it usually offers much lower variance.

Note: To estimate the forward KL value ⟦INLMATH188MATHEND⟧, but only sample from ⟦INLMATH189MATHEND⟧, use importance sampling ⟦INLMATH190MATHEND⟧.

5. Two Ways to Use a KL Penalty

Having understood the value properties of these estimators, we need to further clarify: How exactly is the KL penalty applied in reinforcement learning? This choice determines whether we only care about the estimator’s value properties, or must also consider its gradient properties.

Recall the objective for KL-regularized reinforcement learning (where ⟦INLMATH191MATHEND⟧ denotes the trajectory distribution induced by policy ⟦INLMATH192MATHEND⟧):

⟦DISPMATH21MATHEND⟧

This mathematical form looks unified, but in actor-critic algorithms (e.g., PPO) it gives rise to two fundamentally different implementation paradigms. They often differ by only a few lines of code, yet correspond to different optimization semantics.

Notation: In this section, we use ⟦INLMATH193MATHEND⟧ or ⟦INLMATH194MATHEND⟧ to generically refer to a token/state-level KL estimator (such as ⟦INLMATH195MATHEND⟧), with specific definitions from the earlier section “Three Estimators: Definitions and Design Principles”.

5.1 As a Loss Term: KL Participates in Backpropagation

actor_loss = -advantage * log_prob + beta * kl  # kl participates in gradient

The critic learns only the environment value function; the KL term acts as an explicit regularizer for the actor and participates directly in backpropagation.

5.2 As a Reward Penalty: KL Enters Reward Shaping

kl = compute_kl(log_prob_q, log_prob_p).detach()
shaped_reward = reward - beta * kl

KL is treated as part of the reward via reward shaping, and the actor-critic update is performed on the shaped reward. The KL term itself is detached and does not backpropagate.

These two approaches may look like they differ only by a .detach(), but they correspond to different optimization semantics. A detailed comparison appears later in “⟦INLMATH196MATHEND⟧ in Reward vs. Low-Variance KL in Loss: Equivalence and Differences”. Here we first summarize the core distinction:

  • KL as a loss term: Requires correct gradients for the KL component, including which objective those gradients correspond to.
  • KL as a reward penalty: Requires accurate KL values, and also requires that the induced policy-gradient update matches the intended objective.

Below we analyze estimator gradients under the two usage modes: as a differentiable loss term and as a detached reward penalty.
